topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom glittered under a thousand crystal chandeliers, a dazzling spectacle that only amplified the knot tightening in my chest. I had deliberately chosen this venue, a place where I knew I’d run into him, just to prove a point to myself. He was across the room, laughing with a group of old college friends, looking every bit the successful man I’d spent the last few years trying to outpace. When his eyes finally swept across the room and landed on mine, a flicker of something—recognition, maybe regret—crossed his face before he quickly turned back to his conversation. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and I pivoted sharply toward the buffet table, pretending the sudden heat rising in my cheeks was just the champagne. I needed distance. I needed to keep moving, to show him, and more importantly, myself, that I was perfectly fine without him in the frame.

[story 2]

I smoothed down the fabric of my dress for the tenth time, a small, repetitive motion that anchored me against the sudden, unwelcome awareness of his presence near the entrance. When I saw him, leaning against a marble pillar, the air seemed to thin out. My breath hitched, a tiny, involuntary sound I immediately tried to swallow down. He looked… unchanged, which somehow made the encounter more irritating. I took a deep, steadying breath, focusing intently on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper, refusing to let my gaze linger. I navigated the crowd with practiced indifference, my pace deliberately brisk, my shoulders held back stiffly. I made it to the edge of the dance floor, catching a glimpse of him one last time—a brief, sharp acknowledgment—and then I turned my attention entirely to the conversation happening beside me, shutting the door firmly on any potential reconnection.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave of joy washing over the room, but all I could focus on was the rigid set of my jaw. I was waiting for a moment when I could slip away, to find a quiet corner where the pressure of this forced proximity wouldn't crush me. Then, there he was. Standing near the floral arrangements, looking entirely too relaxed amidst the structured elegance. A strange tension coiled low in my stomach, a resistance building up, a refusal to acknowledge the history that connected us. I didn't break stride; instead, I walked with a deliberate, measured pace, my eyes fixed straight ahead, refusing to give him the satisfaction of seeing any reaction. If he looked, I would look away, and I would continue my journey through the festivities as if he were merely an irrelevant shadow in the periphery.
